purpose: System-level guide that defines what a good rulebook looks like. All component rulebooks must conform to this guide. The guide itself improves over time through jobs post-mortem (system-level changes).

id.description: Unique rule ID. Prefix matches the component slug (e.g., ARB for agent-role-builder, LTB for llm-tool-builder, CLS for classifier, INT for intelligence). Sequential numbering.

rule.description: The generalized principle in one sentence. High-level, not implementation-specific. Should be understandable by someone with zero context about the specific component.
rule.good_example: Define authority as a strict precedence chain with explicit layers, not a flat list of superiors.
rule.bad_example: Fix the authority section. (too vague, no principle)

applies_to.description: Array of specific sections, files, or areas of the artifact where this rule is relevant. Helps the implementer know WHERE to check.

do.description: Concrete instruction of what compliant behavior looks like. Include a specific example of correct implementation. The implementer should be able to copy/adapt this.
requirements: Must start with an actionable verb (define, list, check, validate, separate, use, state)
Must include at least one concrete example of what compliance looks like
Must reference specific artifact sections or code locations where applicable

dont.description: Concrete instruction of what violation looks like. Include a specific example of what NOT to do. The implementer should recognize this pattern and avoid it.
requirements: Must describe the specific anti-pattern, not just negate the 'do'
Must include at least one concrete example of the violation
Must explain why it's wrong (what downstream problem it causes)

source.description: Which review cycle(s) produced this rule. References specific runs and what was observed. Provides credibility - this rule exists because a real failure happened.
source.example: Runs 003-006: authority modeling flagged in every run. Reviewers consistently rejected flat subordinate_to lists.

version.description: Integer version number. Incremented when the rule is updated. History preserved in git.

supersedes.description: ID of rule this replaces, if applicable. The superseded rule stays in git history.

severity.description: How critical this rule is. Helps prioritize during compliance checking.

required_fields.component: The component this rulebook belongs to (e.g., 'agent-role-builder')
required_fields.rules: Array of rule objects conforming to rule_schema

what_rules_are: Distilled from real review failures - not invented speculatively
Actionable by a first-time implementer with no prior context
Stable across runs - a rule that only applies to one specific artifact version is not a rule
what_rules_are_not: Not copies of reviewer feedback ('round 2 said X')
Not implementation details ('add null check on line 47')
Not temporary fixes ('workaround for encoding bug')
quality_checklist: Can a first-time implementer understand this rule without reading the review history?
Does the 'do' example show exactly what compliance looks like?
Does the 'dont' example show exactly what violation looks like?
Is the rule general enough to apply across runs, not just one specific draft?
Is the rule specific enough that compliance can be mechanically verified?
Does the source field prove this rule emerged from a real failure?

routing_policy.purpose: Route each learned finding to the narrowest authority surface that can actually enforce it.

destinations.component_rulebook: Recurring component-local directive that an implementer can mechanically self-check during authoring or repair.
destinations.component_contract: Machine-enforced invariant, required input, output, or terminal-state rule specific to one component bundle.
destinations.shared_contract: Machine-enforced invariant or vocabulary shared across multiple components or review domains.
destinations.doc_only: Human-facing rationale, lifecycle explanation, or design authority note that is not itself a machine check.

routing_questions: Can the runtime, schema, or validator enforce this directly? If yes, it belongs in a contract, not a rulebook.
Is this mainly rationale, sequencing explanation, or authority narrative for humans? If yes, it belongs in docs, not a rulebook.
Is this a recurring component-local directive that an implementer can mechanically walk during self-check? If yes, it belongs in the component rulebook.
Does an existing rule or contract already cover the failure? If yes, rewrite, merge, or supersede instead of appending a near-duplicate rule.

system_rules.rule: Every learned finding must be routed to the narrowest authority surface that can mechanically enforce it: component rulebook, component contract, shared contract, or doc-only guidance.
system_rules.source: 2026-03-29 governance split review: overlap came from contract/process policy living inside rulebooks.

system_rules.rule: Before adding a new active rule, check whether an existing rule can be rewritten, merged, or superseded so the rulebook does not accumulate overlapping guidance.
system_rules.source: 2026-03-29 governance split review: overlapping authority, artifact, and error-handling rules reduced maintainability.

lifecycle: Rules are created by the learning engine from review feedback
Rules are never silently deleted - history preserved in git
Rules can be superseded by a new version with 'supersedes' field
The rulebook is updated at the end of every review round (run post-mortem)
When a learned finding is really a contract or documentation gap, update that authority surface instead of appending a rulebook entry
The rule book guide itself is updated during jobs post-mortem (system-level improvement)
